data.title: CoreWeave, Nebius in spotlight as BNP discusses capacity vs. sustainability debate
data.description:
CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS) were in the spotlight on Thursday as investment firm BNP Paribas weighed in on the debate of providing compute capacity versus long-term sustainability.

“Near term, we continue to find the pricing environment supportive, evidenced in part by SpaceX’s (SPCX) recent AI infrastructure deals with Anthropic and Google, which we estimate generates $30 – 50bn of revenue/GW (it’s unclear where the $6bn Reflection AI deal lands on a revenue/GW basis for SpaceX),” BNP analyst Stefan Slowinski wrote in a note to clients. “While we believe SpaceX’s economics are at least partially driven by the company’s ability to deliver near-term compute to two arguably price-insensitive customers, we still view the deals as indicative of a positive pricing backdrop.”

Delving deeper, Slowinski said the concern around the shift to token optimization from so-called “toxxenmaxxing” is likely to help Nebius, even as demand is still “well ahead of supply.” (Slowinski pointed to the fact that Amazon Web Services (AMZN) recently announced 20% price increases for its GPU reservation service.)

“In this regard, Nebius screens relatively well as its AI cloud stack appears better positioned to support fine-tuned open-source models for enterprise customers seeking cheaper alternatives to frontier models (and frontier lab pricing), adding yet another leg to the stock’s broader narrative (even if this has yet to manifest into reported results),” Slowinski added.

For the larger neocloud sector, Slowinski said the group can accept lower unlevered returns when compared to the hyperscalers given their stacks are still early in their monetization and their financial leverage can enable “attractive project-level levered IRRs.”

Concerning CoreWeave's share price underperformance compared to Nebius, Slowinski said he sees a “potential catch-up opportunity” as execution continued to improve in the second half of 2026.

“The company is adding significant capacity, which masks the underlying unit economics of the business, while newer contracts are being priced to offset component inflation while maintaining mid-20% contribution margins,” Slowinski explained. “Moreover, CoreWeave is simply further along in its capacity build relative to its neocloud peers (~1 GW of active power vs. ~200 MW at Nebius) and could soon reach a point where margin volatility moderates as net new deployments become a smaller percentage of the existing installed base. With a favorable pricing backdrop, effective use of asset-backed leverage, and a broadly declining cost of capital, we continue to view the risk/reward as attractive at these levels.”

Slowinski is still “fundamentally positive” on Nebius, but given the sharp run in the stock this year, he has a Neutral rating on its shares.

data.title: Goldman Sachs raises airlines' outlook, downgrades SkyWest on block hour guidance
data.description:
Goldman Sachs is raising its outlook on the airline industry as strong demand for air travel has yet to be blunted by increased fares to compensate for higher fuel prices.

Accordingly, the firm raised its Q3 and Q4 2026 industry net income forecast by 24% and 32%, respectively.

“We believe revenue trends have been bolstered by the industry’s quick response to moderate supply and the improvement in the competitive environment following Spirit’s cessation of service in May,” Goldman analyst Catherine O’Brien said in her note to clients.

O’Brien singles out Allegiant Travel Company (ALGT) for its post-Sun Country merger outlook, noting the combined company’s “significantly improved revenue trends.”

“We expect strong revenue trends at Allegiant to continue as it benefits from lower leisure-focused capacity and ramping merger synergies,” O’Brien says, reiterating her Buy rating on the carrier and raising Allegiant’s (ALGT) price target by 14% to $142.

O’Brien also raised the target price for Alaska Air (ALK) by 19% to $69, American Airlines (AAL) by 50% to $15, Delta (DAL) by 45% to $116, Sell-rated JetBlue (JBLU) by 28% to $4.50, Southwest (LUV) by 17%, and United (UAL) by 24% to $162.

The outlier is SkyWest (SKYW), which O’Brien downgraded to Neutral from Buy with a 14% reduction to its price target (now at $108) on increased downside risk to block hour production.

SkyWest’s (SKYW) business is primarily driven by long-term capacity purchase agreements with its partner airlines, measured as block hours. During the first quarter earnings call, management indicated it expects block hour production will be “slightly” lower this summer than modeled for last quarter. 

This led O’Brien to caution that while fuel prices have come down materially from the April peak, “they remain elevated vs. pre-war levels, and we therefore expect industry capacity growth to remain lower than initially expected over the medium term, increasing downside risk to SkyWest’s block hour production.”

O’Brien lowered her 2026 block hour growth forecast to 3.0% from 3.5%, previously, and from 4.9% earlier this year, representing a “significant deceleration” from recent years.  

SkyWest (SKYW) shares are lower on Thursday, currently challenging support at the 200-day moving average.

data.title: Anthropic considers building in-house AI chip with Samsung as key partner: report
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) is considering building an in-house AI chip and is in discussions with Samsung (SSNLF) as a potential manufacturing partner in the project, according to The Information.

If the potential deal becomes finalized, Anthropic would use Samsung's foundry business and 2-nanometer technology, the report said, citing sources with knowledge on the matter.

In recent years, several big tech companies outside the semiconductor sector have turned to developing in-house, custom silicon. Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) all build lines of in-house AI chips.

Last week, Anthropic's chief rival, OpenAI (OPENAI), revealed Jalapeño, a new chip designed specifically to run inference for large language models. They partnered with Broadcom (AVGO) and Celestica (CLS) on the project.

data.title: AI data centers are reshaping the energy infrastructure landscape
data.description:
Investors Should Know: AI data center expansion is driving fresh demand for power and energy infrastructure, putting a diverse group of utilities and energy suppliers in focus for investors tracking the buildout.

Background

The rapid growth of AI data centers has created a surge in electricity demand that existing grid infrastructure was not built to handle. Power-hungry computing facilities require reliable, large-scale energy supply — and that need is pulling capital toward a broad range of energy providers.

The suppliers positioned to meet this demand span multiple energy types: fuel cell technology, nuclear power, natural gas generation, and traditional electric utilities. Each offers a different risk and return profile, but all share exposure to the same underlying driver — the need for more power, delivered reliably, at scale.

Stocks to Watch

Bloom Energy Corporation (BE) is classified as a core AI power supplier, with a year-to-date return of 248% reflecting strong market interest in its fuel cell technology. Talen Energy Corporation (TLN) and Oklo Inc. (OKLO) represent the nuclear side of the equation, with Talen focused on operating generation assets and Oklo developing next-generation small modular reactors.

American Electric Power Company (AEP), one of the largest U.S. electric utilities by market cap at roughly $74 billion, also carries the AI power supplier classification. The AES Corporation (AES), TransAlta Corporation (TAC), Capital Power Corp. (CPXWF), Fermi Inc. (FRMI), Capstone Energy+, Inc. (CGEH), and Power Solutions International (PSIX) round out the thematic universe.

AI Power Infrastructure in Focus

The screener below captures stocks classified under the AI power and energy infrastructure theme, with companies ordered by how closely they align with the AI bottlenecks — power and energy theme, placing the most theme-relevant names first.

data.title: SEC looks into alleged insider trades on China's brokerage crackdown - report
data.description:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission is investigating claims made by Susquehanna International Group that unidentified insider traders reaped $100M on option bets placed before Chinese authorities cracked down on cross-border brokerages, according to a media report.

Susquehanna made the claims in a lawsuit filed Monday. The SEC is now looking at the trades described in the market-making company's complaint, Bloomberg News reported late Wednesday, citing a person familiar with the matter.

In the suit against 100 John Doe defendants, Susquehanna said it lost more than $70M as a counterparty on most of the alleged insider trades. The firm said it doesn't know who made the trades but said "the high-risk, high-reward" option bets could only be plausibly explained as insider trading.

On Monday, a federal judge granted Susquehanna's request to freeze accounts at Interactive Brokers (IBKR), Futu Holdings (FUTU), and Up Fintech Holdings (TIGR), platforms that the defendants allegedly used to make the trades. 

Up Fintech's (TIGR) Tiger Brokers and  Futu (FUTU) were also subjects of the Chinese government's investigation into offshore brokerages operating unlicensed trading services for mainland China residents.

A spokesperson for Interactive Brokers (IBKR) told Bloomberg it's cooperating with Susquehanna and will cooperate with relevant regulators.

Interactive Brokers (IBKR) stock rose 0.6% in Thursday morning trading. Futu (FUTU) fell 0.7%, and UP Fintech (TIGR) slipped 0.7%.

data.title: 10 least shorted U.S. tech stocks above $2B market cap
data.description:
While high short interest often grabs investors' attention, some of the biggest names in technology continue to attract little bearish positioning. Low short interest typically reflects relatively positive investor sentiment toward a stock. It can also reflect strong institutional ownership, stable fundamentals, and broad market confidence.

The latest data shows a mix of communications equipment, semiconductor, and software companies dominating the list of the least shorted U.S. technology stocks with market capitalizations above $2B, signaling relatively positive investor sentiment.

The least shorted U.S. technology stocks with market capitalizations above $2B are:

  • Ubiquiti (UI) – Communications Equipment: 0.78% short interest
  • Apple (AAPL) – Technology Hardware, Storage & Peripherals: 0.98%
  • GlobalFoundries (GFS) – Semiconductors: 1.16%
  • Nvidia (NVDA) – Semiconductors: 1.24%
  • Microsoft (MSFT) – Systems Software: 1.28%
  • Broadcom (AVGO) – Semiconductors: 1.29%
  • Oracle (ORCL) – Systems Software: 1.31%
  • Amphenol (APH) – Electronic Components: 1.40%
  • Cisco Systems (CSCO) – Communications Equipment: 1.57%
  • Arista Networks (ANET) – Communications Equipment: 1.60%

Related technology ETFs: XLK, VGT, IYW, FTEC, SMH, SOXX, IGV, SKYY, AIQ, and QQQ.

data.title: Datadog's moat should 'thrive in AI super cycle': Benchmark
data.description:
Datadog (DDOG), which provides observability and security for cloud applications, has built a "technological moat to thrive in the AI super cycle," according to Benchmark analyst Yi Fu Lee.

"Our recent observation concludes Datadog is competitively positioned against peer leaders of the observability platform space, well supported by an AI-powered, innovative technology roadmap that is forward-thinking, as exemplified by the generous organic research & development budget clearing >$1 billion in 2025," Lee said in a Thursday investor note.

Benchmark reiterated its Buy rating and increased its price target to $330 from $260. 

Datadog has surged more than 90% year to date. The rapid share value gain was supported by Datadog's first quarter results, which featured a 32% year-over-year revenue jump and a raised outlook.

"Our observations from DASH 2026 reinforced the view that Datadog's leadership team remains singularly focused on expanding the platform's role well beyond traditional observability," Lee noted.

During its DASH 2026 conference earlier this month, Datadog announced more than 100 new capabilities centered around autonomous operations, AI agents, security, developer productivity, data observability, and infrastructure monitoring.

"Most notably, Datadog is positioning Bits AI as the foundation of an emerging autonomous AI operations control plane capable of detecting, investigating, and ultimately remediating issues across production environments," Lee noted. "We view this as an important strategic evolution as enterprise customers transition from monitoring applications to operating increasingly complex AI-driven systems."

data.title: 30 Russell 1000 stocks doubled in 2026
data.description:
Thirty Russell 1000 (IWB) stocks have already gained more than 100% in 2026, with the biggest winner surging an extraordinary 763.8% just six months into the year.

Four stocks have returned more than 250%, delivering gains financial analyst Grant Hawkridge described as those "you normally associate with a market that lacks leadership."

 

 

 

Technology companies dominate the list of top performers, with 24 of the 30 gainers coming from the tech sector. Digging deeper, semiconductors and their supporting industries make up the bulk of winners, as "chip designers, manufacturers, equipment makers, and materials suppliers are all represented," according to Hawkridge.

The data challenges the prevailing narrative that the AI boom has been driven primarily by Nvidia (NVDA) and the Magnificent Seven (MAGS), the analyst noted.

This "tells a different story," where the biggest winners are "companies that receive far less attention but play a critical role in building the hardware and infrastructure behind AI."

Money has flowed across the entire AI ecosystem regardless of company size, with winners ranging from businesses worth less than $10B to those valued at more than $1T, Hawkridge observed.

Hardware has quietly emerged as one of the biggest winners, with Sandisk (SNDK), Western Digital (WDC) and Dell (DELL) all sitting “near the top of the leaderboard," he noted, as AI drives demand for storage, servers, networking, and infrastructure.

Bull markets create dozens of “monster winners” rather than just one or two, the strategist concluded.

He urged investors to maintain perspective, writing that "the best opportunities aren't always the biggest companies or the stocks making headlines. Sometimes they're the businesses quietly benefiting from the same theme."

data.title: UBS already has enough capital to meet proposed new rules - Swiss central bank
data.description:
UBS Group (UBS) already has sufficient capital to meet the proposed new rules, according to the Swiss central bank.

A proposed legislation in Switzerland requires UBS to fully back its foreign units with ​common equity Tier 1 capital. The new rule is intended to toughen legislation to prevent a repeat of the Credit Suisse meltdown.

The new rule was anticipated to require UBS to strengthen its CET1 capital by ~$20B. The Swiss bank was opposing the proposal, saying that they would hurt its ability to compete globally.

Notably, the Financial Stability Report 2026 released by the Swiss National Bank today said, "UBS exceeds its fully applied capital requirements under the current 'too big to fail' ((TBTF)) regulations applicable as of 2030."

"Regarding liquidity, experience from 2022 – 2023 in Switzerland and the U.S. shows that outflows can deplete liquidity buffers rapidly, even if these buffers are substantial. Some banks may also face the risk of liquidity shortfalls in foreign currencies. It is therefore essential that banks prepare sufficient collateral eligible for accessing liquidity support from the SNB (Extended Liquidity Facility, ELF) and, where applicable, from foreign central banks," said the report.

"The proposal of the Federal Council addresses these risks in a targeted manner and primarily affects UBS," said the report.

"According to the pro forma calculations of the authorities and including reserves, UBS already has sufficient capital to meet the proposed requirements," said the report.

data.title: U.S. natural gas inventory net change of +87 Bcf for week ended June 26 – EIA
data.description:
• Stocks for the week ended June 26 were 2,922 Bcf vs. 2,835 Bcf for the week ended June 19.
